perturbation.original_text: (2) "Non-Competition Period" means the period commencing on May 12, ---------------------- 1999 and ending on May 11, 2000.
perturbation.changed_text: (2) "Non-Competition Period" means the period commencing on May 12, ---------------------- 1999 and ending on May 11, 2001.
perturbation.explanation: By extending the Non-Competition Period to May 11, 2001, we introduce a contradiction. Later sections of the contract (e.g., Article III) rely on the original Non-Competition Period definition ending in 2000. This change affects the duration of the non-compete obligations, creating uncertainty about the period during which Seller and CompuCom are restricted from certain activities. This change would require other provisions in the agreement to be adapted, which now creates a contradiction.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3.1 Seller's Agreement. (a) Seller agrees that during the Non- ------------------ Competition Period Seller will not, and will not permit any of its Subsidiaries to: (i) engage in the Product Business; (ii) encourage any Service Account to specify a party other than CompuCom to provide Products and image load/configurations to such Service Account; or (iii) solicit, entice or induce any employee of CompuCom or any Subsidiary of CompuCom to terminate his or her employment with CompuCom or any Subsidiary of CompuCom or hire any person who was or is at any time from the date of execution of the Asset Purchase Agreement to the end of the Non-Competition Period an employee of CompuCom or any Subsidiary of CompuCom.
perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the 'Non-Competition Period' from ending in May 11, 2000 to May 11, 2001. The contradicted text, Section 3.1, references the 'Non-Competition Period' in defining the duration of Seller's agreement not to engage in certain activities. By extending the period, the changed text creates a direct conflict because Section 3.1 is now based on an outdated definition of the Non-Competition Period, thereby creating uncertainty about the enforcement of non-compete obligations.

perturbation.original_text: (4) "Product Business" means the acceptance and fulfillment of ---------------- customer orders for Products, including the manufacturing, channel assembly, co-location or centralized image loading and Configuration of Products; provided that the term "Product Business" shall not include on- -------- site Configuration of computer or related <PAGE> hardware or software products made subsequent to the initial image load/configuration.
perturbation.changed_text: (4) "Product Business" means only the acceptance and fulfillment of ---------------- customer orders for Products, excluding the manufacturing, channel assembly, co-location or centralized image loading and Configuration of Products; provided that the term "Product Business" shall not include on- -------- site Configuration of computer or related <PAGE> hardware or software products made subsequent to the initial image load/configuration.
perturbation.explanation: By changing the definition of "Product Business" to exclude manufacturing, channel assembly, co-location or centralized image loading and Configuration, we create a contradiction with other sections of the agreement (e.g., Article III) where those activities are implicitly considered part of the Product Business when outlining non-compete obligations. This change introduces ambiguity as to what activities Seller is restricted from engaging in.This is because Article III, Section 3.1 refers to the "Product Business" when detailing the non-compete agreement, therefore, this definition change would cause confusion
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3.1 Seller's Agreement. (a) Seller agrees that during the Non- ------------------ Competition Period Seller will not, and will not permit any of its Subsidiaries to: (i) engage in the Product Business; (ii) encourage any Service Account to specify a party other than CompuCom to provide Products and image load/configurations to such Service Account; or (iii) solicit, entice or induce any employee of CompuCom or any Subsidiary of CompuCom to terminate his or her employment with CompuCom or any Subsidiary of CompuCom or hire any person who was or is at any time from the date of execution of the Asset Purchase Agreement to the end of the Non-Competition Period an employee of CompuCom or any Subsidiary of CompuCom.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the definition of 'Product Business' to exclude 'manufacturing, channel assembly, co-location or centralized image loading and Configuration of Products'. The contradicted text (Section 3.1) refers to the 'Product Business' when outlining non-compete obligations. Because the definition of Product Business now excludes key activities, the scope of the non-compete agreement in Section 3.1 becomes ambiguous, creating a direct conflict in how the term 'Product Business' is understood and applied in the contract. This aligns with the definition of 'Inconsistencies - In Text Contradiction'.

perturbation.original_text: (7) "Services" means all IT services offered by Seller, including, -------- but not limited to, all outsourcing, professional services, break/fix, staff augmentation and consulting services; provided that the term -------- "Services" shall not include (i) on-site Configuration of Products by CompuCom or (ii) the sale (but not the performance) by CompuCom of extended warranty contracts at time of initial sale of Products to customers.
perturbation.changed_text: (7) "Services" means only break/fix and staff augmentation services; provided that the term -------- "Services" shall not include (i) on-site Configuration of Products by CompuCom or (ii) the sale (but not the performance) by CompuCom of extended warranty contracts at time of initial sale of Products to customers.
perturbation.explanation: By limiting the definition of "Services" to only break/fix and staff augmentation, we create a conflict with Article III where CompuCom's non-compete obligations include refraining from offering "Services" to certain accounts. This narrower definition creates an ambiguity as to whether CompuCom is permitted to offer outsourcing, professional and consulting services to those accounts, due to the change in definition of services. This creates uncertainty about the scope of CompuCom's non-compete obligations, specifically in Article III, section 3.2.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3.2 CompuCom's Agreement. (a) CompuCom agrees that during the Non- -------------------- Competition Period CompuCom will not, and will not permit any of its Subsidiaries to: (i) provide any Services to any account listed on Exhibit B hereto; --------- (ii) provide any Services to any account listed on Exhibit C hereto except to the extent provided for in such Exhibit; or (iii) except as contemplated by the Asset Purchase Agreement, solicit, entice or induce any employee of Seller or any Subsidiary of Seller to terminate his or her employment with Seller or any Subsidiary of Seller or hire any person who was or is at any time from the date of execution of the Asset Purchase Agreement to the end of the Non-Competition Period an employee of Seller or any Subsidiary of Seller.
perturbation.justification: The original text defines 'Services' broadly, including IT services like outsourcing, professional services, etc. The changed text narrows the definition of 'Services' to only break/fix and staff augmentation services. Article 3.2 states that CompuCom will not provide any 'Services' to accounts listed in Exhibit B or C. Because the definition of 'Services' has been narrowed, it creates a contradiction. It's unclear whether CompuCom is allowed to offer outsourcing, professional services, or consulting services to those accounts listed in Exhibit B or C. This creates a conflict between the limited definition in section (7) and the obligation in Article 3.2. This is an inconsistency that leads to an in-text contradiction, where different sections of a contract provide conflicting obligations.

perturbation.original_text: ARTICLE II AGREEMENT TO COOPERATE Subject to the limitations set forth in Article III, Seller and CompuCom agree that with respect to each account listed on Exhibit A hereto, --------- that until the earlier of (a) May 11, 2000 or (b) the termination of the existing contract between Seller and such account (i) each party will cooperate with the other in delivering Services and Products to such account in substantially the same manner in which such Services and Products were delivered to such -2- <PAGE> account prior to Closing and (ii) each party's representative will be permitted to call on such account.
perturbation.changed_text: ARTICLE II AGREEMENT TO COOPERATE Subject to the limitations set forth in Article III, Seller and CompuCom agree that with respect to each account listed on Exhibit A hereto, --------- that until the earlier of (a) May 11, 2000 or (b) the termination of the existing contract between Seller and such account (i) only Seller will cooperate with the other in delivering Services and Products to such account in substantially the same manner in which such Services and Products were delivered to such -2- <PAGE> account prior to Closing and (ii) each party's representative will be permitted to call on such account.
perturbation.explanation: By restricting the cooperation in delivering Services and Products to only Seller, and excluding CompuCom, we introduce a contradiction. Article II originally mandated mutual cooperation. This change creates uncertainty for the accounts in Exhibit A. This is contradicting the spirit and intent in Article II, where it states that "Seller and CompuCom agree".
perturbation.contradicted_text: ARTICLE II AGREEMENT TO COOPERATE Subject to the limitations set forth in Article III, Seller and CompuCom agree that with respect to each account listed on Exhibit A hereto, --------- that until the earlier of (a) May 11, 2000 or (b) the termination of the existing contract between Seller and such account (i) only Seller will cooperate with the other in delivering Services and Products to such account in substantially the same manner in which such Services and Products were delivered to such -2- <PAGE> account prior to Closing and (ii) each party's representative will be permitted to call on such account.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies Article II to state that 'only Seller will cooperate', which directly contradicts the original agreement in Article II that 'Seller and CompuCom agree that...each party will cooperate'. This creates a clear and direct conflict regarding which party is responsible for cooperation, thereby creating an in-text contradiction, and aligning with the definition of 'Inconsistencies - In Text Contradiction'. The contradicted text is the same as the changed text, indicating that the change itself introduces a contradiction within Article II.

perturbation.original_text: ARTICLE IV Acceptance of Product Orders CompuCom agrees that during the Non-Competition Period, it will accept orders for Products submitted to CompuCom by Seller pursuant to Section 3.1(b) under the following conditions: (i) CompuCom has the ability to offer such Product for sale; (ii) the purchase price required by the customer order for the Product is reasonably acceptable to CompuCom; (iii) such Product offered by CompuCom meets the customer's specifications, including, but not limited to, configuration specifications; and (iv) the requirements of the customer with respect to such Product, including but not limited to delivery, payment terms and return privileges are reasonably acceptable to CompuCom. CompuCom agrees to notify Seller of CompuCom's rejection of such orders for Product within a mutually agreed upon time that is reasonable under the time constraints required by the customer order or contract.
perturbation.changed_text: ARTICLE IV Acceptance of Product Orders CompuCom agrees that during the Non-Competition Period, it is not obligated to accept orders for Products submitted to CompuCom by Seller pursuant to Section 3.1(b). CompuCom is only required to use commercially reasonable efforts to fulfill the referred customer needs. CompuCom agrees to notify Seller of CompuCom's rejection of such orders for Product within a mutually agreed upon time that is reasonable under the time constraints required by the customer order or contract.
perturbation.explanation: By stating that CompuCom is 'not obligated' to accept orders, we are directly contradicting the initial statement that it 'will accept orders'. This creates an immediate conflict within Article IV. The remainder of Article IV now refers to conditions that are not required, which creates an uncertainty and internal contradiction. The 'shall' in Article IV is changing to 'not obligated'.
perturbation.contradicted_text: ARTICLE IV Acceptance of Product Orders CompuCom agrees that during the Non-Competition Period, it is not obligated to accept orders for Products submitted to CompuCom by Seller pursuant to Section 3.1(b). CompuCom is only required to use commercially reasonable efforts to fulfill the referred customer needs. CompuCom agrees to notify Seller of CompuCom's rejection of such orders for Product within a mutually agreed upon time that is reasonable under the time constraints required by the customer order or contract.
perturbation.justification: The original text states that CompuCom 'will accept orders'. The changed text states that CompuCom 'is not obligated to accept orders'. This creates a direct and strong contradiction within Article IV. The subsequent references to conditions for accepting orders are now undermined, as CompuCom is no longer obligated to accept them in the first place, causing uncertainty and internal contradiction. This adheres to the definition of 'Inconsistencies - In Text Contradiction'.
